translatedText: Traveled to the Far East, the United States, etc.
Published the novella The Man Who Would Be King.
Returned to England.
Published the poems Mandalay and Gunga Din.
Published the short story collection Life's Handicap.
Welcomed for Barrack-Room Ballads, depicting military life in India.
Revered as a patriotic poet with The Seven Seas.
Published the novel Kim.
Published Just So Stories.
Became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Wrote the poem If— for his 12-year-old son.
January 18, 1936
Died.
Nobel Prize in Literature
Became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1907, and remains its youngest recipient to date.
Contribution to Children's Literature
Contributed to children's literature by writing fairy tales such as The Jungle Book and Just So Stories.
Pioneering the Short Story
Regarded as a major innovator in the art of the short story.
Works in Various Genres
Recognized for his literary talent by publishing many works across various genres, including novels, poetry, and short stories.
What kind of writer is Rudyard Kipling known as?
He is an English novelist and poet, well known as the author of children's books such as The Jungle Book.
When did Rudyard Kipling win the Nobel Prize in Literature?
He became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1907.
What are Rudyard Kipling's representative works?
His novels include The Jungle Book, Just So Stories, and Kim, and his poems include Mandalay and Gunga Din.
In what literary field is Rudyard Kipling considered a pioneer?
He is regarded as a major innovator in the art of the short story.
Born in England (June 13).
Entered Oxford University to study modern languages and medieval literature.
Received a Master of Arts degree from Oxford University, becoming the first woman to receive a degree from Oxford at the time.
Published her first novel, Whose Body?.
Formed the Detection Club in England with G. K. Chesterton, Agatha Christie, Ronald Knox, and others.
Died (December 17).
Publication of First Novel Whose Body?
Published her first novel Whose Body? in 1923, featuring her persona, Lord Peter Wimsey, as a detective.
First Woman to Receive a Degree from Oxford University
Received a Master of Arts degree from Oxford University in 1920, becoming the first woman to receive an Oxford degree at the time.
Writing the Lord Peter Wimsey Series
Wrote a detective novel series featuring Lord Peter Wimsey for 15 years, evaluated as masterpieces representing the Golden Age of Detective Fiction.
Formation of the Detection Club
Formed the Detection Club in England in 1929 along with G. K. Chesterton, Agatha Christie, and others.
Writing Across Various Fields
Engaged in writing across a very broad range of fields until right before her death, including detective novels, poetry, plays, literary criticism, translation, and essays.
Authorship of The Zeal for Thy House
Wrote The Zeal for Thy House, a representative work containing Christian theological lessons.
In what fields was Dorothy L. Sayers active?
She was a detective novelist, poet, and advertising copywriter, and wrote across a wide range of fields including plays, literary criticism, essays, and translation.
What are her representative works?
Her representative works are the detective novel series featuring her persona Lord Peter Wimsey, and The Zeal for Thy House, which contains Christian lessons.
What is Dorothy L. Sayers' connection to Oxford University?

She entered Oxford University in 1912 to study modern languages and medieval literature, and in 1920, she received a Master of Arts degree, becoming the first woman to earn an Oxford degree at the time.
With which writers did she associate?
She built friendships with prominent writers of her time such as C. S. Lewis, J. R. R. Tolkien, and T. S. Eliot, and formed the Detection Club with Agatha Christie and others.
September 13, 1819
Born Clara Josephine Wieck in Leipzig.
Her parents divorced.
October 28, 1828
Made her official debut at the Gewandhaus in Leipzig.
Met Robert Schumann.
Composed Quatre Polonaises pour le pianoforte, Op. 1.
September 1831 - April 1832
Toured Paris and other European cities with her father.
Performed her Piano Concerto in A minor with the Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra conducted by Mendelssohn.
Accepted Robert Schumann's proposal of marriage.
March 15, 1838
Appointed Royal and Imperial Austrian Chamber Virtuoso, Austria's highest musical honor.
September 12, 1840
Married composer Robert Schumann.
Collaborated with Robert Schumann on a song cycle based on Friedrich Rückert's Liebesfrühling (Love's Spring).
November 1844
Met violinist Joseph Joachim for the first time.
December 4, 1845
Premiered Robert Schumann's Piano Concerto in Dresden.
Composed Piano Trio in G minor, Op. 17.
May 1849
Rescued her children during the May Uprising in Dresden.
Met Johannes Brahms.
Composed Three Romances for Violin and Piano, Op. 22.
February 1854
Robert Schumann suffered a mental breakdown, attempted suicide, and was committed to an asylum.
April 1856
Toured England for the first time.
Her husband Robert Schumann died.
Premiered Brahms's Variations and Fugue on a Theme by Handel for solo piano.
Continued her sixteen annual winter-spring tours of England.
Commissioned by a publisher to transcribe 41 of Robert Schumann's lieder.
Appointed as the first piano teacher at the newly founded Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt.
Performed Beethoven's Choral Fantasy with the newly formed Berlin Philharmonic.
Edited the early letters (Jugendbriefe) of her husband, Robert Schumann.
Edited Robert Schumann's piano works by adding fingerings and other instructions.
March 12, 1891
Gave her last public concert in Frankfurt.
Retained her teaching position.
Died at the age of 76.
An 1835 portrait of her by Andreas Staub was featured on the 100 Deutsche Mark banknote.
Renowned Concert Pianist
Regarded as one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era, exerting her influence through a 61-year concert career.
Innovator of the Piano Recital Format
Changed the format and repertoire of the piano recital by reducing the importance of purely virtuosic works, and made playing from memory the standard for concerts.
Prolific Composer
Composed works in various genres, including solo piano pieces, a piano concerto, chamber music, choral pieces, and lieder.
Influential Piano Educator
Worked as an influential piano educator at Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium in Frankfurt from 1878, attracting international students.
Editor of Robert Schumann's Works
Edited publications of her husband Robert Schumann's works, adding instructions to his letters and piano pieces.
Promoter of Robert Schumann's Music
Worked tirelessly throughout her life to promote her husband Robert Schumann's works so they would be recognized and appreciated.
Who was Clara Schumann?
Clara Schumann was a German pianist, composer, and piano teacher, considered one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era.
When did Clara Schumann marry Robert Schumann?
Clara Schumann married the composer Robert Schumann on September 12, 1840.
What impact did Clara Schumann have on piano performance?
